turns.content: I'm trying to understand why my Rust tutorial says I can't just use a normal function pointer array like in C, but instead needs const fn and trait objects. I thought function pointers were enough for dispatch tables.

The core issue is that regular function pointers don't capture the static guarantee you need for compile-time dispatch tables. In Rust, a const array of functions requires each element to be const fn so the compiler can statically verify the entire table at compile time without runtime checks. This gives you O(1) dispatch because the address calculation happens entirely in the compiler phase rather than requiring dynamic type resolution.
So if I have a Vec of FnMut traits instead, that would be slower? And why do tutorials insist on using trait objects even though they feel like runtime bloat?

Exactly. A Vec of Box<dyn Trait> requires heap allocation and vtable lookup at runtime which kills performance for tight loops or opcode interpreters. The const fn array approach avoids this entirely by eliminating the indirection layer that trait objects introduce. When you name your types correctly like std::array::Array4<fn(i32) -> i32>, the compiler generates a single jump table rather than calling through a dynamic dispatch mechanism.
But what about when I need to add new operations dynamically? Can't I just use trait objects for that part?

You can layer them. Use the const fn array for your stable, frequently executed opcode instructions where performance matters most, then wrap that dispatch table in a trait object container if you need dynamic extensibility. The key insight is separating compile-time known operations from runtime polymorphic ones rather than trying to force everything into one category.
Wait, so I'm mixing const arrays with trait objects? How does that avoid the performance hit?

The performance benefit comes from where the dispatch actually happens. Your hot path uses the const fn array for direct address calculation while the dynamic part only gets invoked when needed through a separate trait object wrapper layer. This means your critical opcode interpreter loop never touches the vtable, keeping that section purely compile-time optimized.
I'm confused about naming - why not just use Fn traits everywhere? Is there something special about const fn specifically?

Fn traits are the trait object equivalent of function pointers, but they don't guarantee constness. The const fn keyword is what enables the entire array to be compiled as a single static structure rather than being treated as runtime polymorphic data. Without that qualifier, your 'dispatch table' becomes just another heap-allocated collection requiring indirection.
Okay so if I want to mimic an opcode interpreter in Rust, what's the minimal structure?

Start with a const array of function pointers matching your specific instruction set. Each element should be a const fn that takes exactly the parameters your opcode expects and returns the expected value type. Then wrap this in a struct that holds the table as its first field so you can access it directly without extra indirection layers.
And what about error handling? Won't trait objects give me better ergonomics for errors?

Error handling becomes explicit in your const fn signatures rather than being delegated through trait bounds. Since each function pointer is statically known, you can match on the result directly without needing to implement complex trait error propagation logic. This forces you to think about error cases upfront which actually improves correctness for opcode interpreters.
This feels like it's making things more complicated than my scripting language approach where I could just use closures dynamically.

The trade-off is that you sacrifice runtime flexibility for compile-time certainty. In scripting languages, dynamic dispatch lets you add features without recompilation but costs performance. Rust's const fn arrays give you fixed instruction sets with zero overhead once compiled, which is why opcode interpreters and game engines prefer this pattern over trait objects.
So the real question is whether I care about that 10% performance difference or if I should just use trait objects for simplicity?

If your interpreter runs millions of instructions per second, that 10% matters significantly and const fn arrays win. But for applications with fewer iterations where code clarity is more important than micro-optimizations, trait objects are acceptable. The choice depends on whether your use case demands the raw speed that only compile-time dispatch tables can provide.

I've read about const fn in arrays and compile-time dispatch tables, but I still don't fully understand why this is better than runtime checks. My current proof-of-concept tooling uses a HashMap to look up handlers based on opcode types, which feels too slow for my low-latency requirements.

The fundamental difference is that your HashMap approach requires dynamic memory allocation and hashing at runtime, whereas const fn in arrays allows the compiler to resolve every function pointer before the program even starts. Think of it like this: your current method is checking a phone book entry for every call, while compile-time dispatch is like having a direct wiring connection from your house to the utility company; there's no searching involved.
That analogy makes sense. But I'm confused about how the compiler actually handles these function pointers in an array without losing type safety or requiring unsafe blocks.

The key is that const fn must be a pure function with no side effects and only take primitive types. When you define an array of such functions, the compiler generates code where each index directly maps to its corresponding function address at compile time. No unsafe blocks are needed because the type system guarantees these pointers are constant.
So I can't use complex traits or generics here? Like if my opcode types come from a trait bound, would that break the const fn requirement?

Exactly right. You cannot put trait objects or generic parameters in this pattern because they require runtime resolution. Your tooling needs to have fixed opcodes defined at compile time with known function signatures matching your handler functions exactly.
I see, so my Rust code should look like an array of fn pointers where each element has a specific signature. But what if I want to extend the opcode set later without recompiling everything?

You would need to add new entries to your const array and recompile, which is actually desirable for safety in critical systems. If you truly need runtime extensibility, consider using a pattern where the opcode itself carries type information so dispatch can happen at compile time based on that metadata.
That sounds promising. Can I still use serde_json::Value for my data structures alongside this? I want to keep JSON parsing separate from the opcode dispatcher.

Yes, absolutely. Your serde_json::Value deserialization can feed into a lookup table that contains simple integer opcodes, which then map to your const fn array. The separation keeps your data handling flexible while maintaining the dispatch performance.
I'm still worried about error cases. What happens if I try to call a function pointer that doesn't exist in my array at runtime?

At compile time, the Rust compiler will catch any mismatch between your opcode and available functions. At runtime, you should add bounds checking before dereferencing these pointers to prevent panics during execution.
This is much clearer now. One last question: does this pattern work well with async/await? I'm building a high-performance event loop in Rust.

Const fn cannot be used directly inside async contexts because they must not contain any await points or side effects. For your event loop, you'd need to separate the compile-time dispatch from the actual async execution where you can use traits and future types instead.
